title: Fake villa booking via Instagram — lost ₹45,000
description: Found a gorgeous villa on Instagram, paid via UPI to a personal number after a 'soft launch' discount. Arrived to find the address was a real house belonging to a confused family who'd never listed it. Lost ₹45,000 with zero recourse. File a cybercrime complaint at cybercrime.gov.in immediately if this happens to you.

title: Royal Enfield 'hidden damage' held my deposit for 4 days
description: Rented from a shop near Anjuna flea market. Photographed the bike beforehand but missed a small scratch on the underside of the mudguard. Owner refused to return ₹5,000 deposit claiming I caused it. Eventually resolved after showing timestamp photos to a police post. Always photograph undersides and wheel arches.

title: Man approached me twice at Vagator — second approach had a 'cop' nearby
description: A man offered hash at Vagator beach, I declined and walked away. He approached again 10 minutes later near a different area, and within seconds a man claiming to be a plainclothes officer appeared demanding I come with him for 'questioning'. I walked directly into the nearest crowded shack and called my guesthouse. Do not stop walking.

title: GoaMiles impersonator at Mopa airport — quoted ₹2,800 for ₹900 ride
description: Man outside Mopa arrivals was holding a card that looked exactly like the GoaMiles logo. Quoted ₹2,800 to Assagao. Actual GoaMiles app shows ₹920 for the same route. He said the 'app doesn't cover Mopa yet' — it does. Only book through the actual app.

title: Auto driver took 45-minute detour to gem 'export warehouse'
description: Agreed ₹100 auto to City Palace. Driver said there was a traffic diversion and took me to a 'government gem export centre' in an industrial area. Multi-person sales team inside. Was there 40 minutes before I managed to leave. No gems bought but shaken. Always use Uber/Ola from monuments.

title: Paid ₹800 for 'skip-the-line' Amber Fort ticket — it was fake
description: A smartly-dressed man outside Amber Fort sold me a ₹800 ticket that included 'fast entry and audio guide'. At the gate the QR code failed and the security guard confirmed it was counterfeit. Had to buy a real ₹200 ticket at the counter. Report: fake tickets are printed convincingly. Only buy at ASI counter or online.

title: Booking.com listing for 'Heritage Haveli' was someone's house
description: The listing had 40 reviews (all 5-star, all posted within 3 weeks). Arrived to find a private home, no reception, no staff. Owner appeared and tried to show me a room — completely unlisted. Got a refund from Booking.com after filing a dispute but lost 2 hours. Check review date patterns before booking.

title: Elephant operator demanded ₹3,500 'tip + feed' after ₹500 agreed price
description: Took an elephant ride up to Amber Fort after agreeing ₹500. At the top, the mahout refused to let me dismount until I paid ₹3,500 for 'feed and tip'. A crowd gathered which increased pressure. Paid ₹500 extra and walked away. Skip elephant rides entirely — jeep taxis to Amber are ₹200 and faster.

title: Fake India Tourism office near Connaught Place — lost 3 hours and ₹12,000
description: A man near Janpath told me India Tourism was closed and walked me to a 'government-approved travel desk' two streets away. Booked a Rajasthan tour for ₹12,000 that included a fake hotel. No tour guide appeared on day 1. The real India Tourism office (88 Janpath) was open the entire time. File complaint at Delhi Police cybercell.

title: Pre-paid taxi booth impersonator at IGI Terminal 2
description: Inside T2 arrivals, a man in a vest that read 'PREPAID TAXI' (not official signage) collected ₹800 for a 'pre-paid slip' and directed me to an unofficial cab. Driver demanded an additional ₹1,500 mid-route. The real prepaid booth is marked with Delhi Traffic Police signage and is to the left of the exit doors.

title: 'Businessman' at Paharganj offered commission to carry gems to London
description: Met a well-dressed man at hotel breakfast who said he was an exporter. Offered £200 to carry a small package of gems to London as 'personal luggage to avoid export duty'. This is illegal gem smuggling and a common Delhi long con. Declined and reported to hotel staff. Never agree to carry anything for anyone.

title: Cycle rickshaw doubled price at destination — refused to move until paid
description: Agreed ₹80 Connaught Place to Janpath. On arrival driver claimed ₹80 was 'per person' (I was alone) and demanded ₹160, then grabbed my bag handle. Shouted once, he let go. A police constable was 30m away — point toward police if this happens. Always state: 'total price, for this journey, right now' before boarding.

title: Silk shop 'student' followed me for 20 minutes from Dashashwamedh Ghat
description: A young man claiming to be a BHU student started a friendly conversation at Dashashwamedh Ghat. Perfectly good English, knowledgeable about the Aarti. Eventually guided me toward a 'family silk shop' a kilometre away. I realised the route only when I checked Maps. The student script is extremely polished in Varanasi — end the conversation early.

title: Sunrise boat ₹200 agreed — ₹2,400 demanded on return, boat moved from ghat
description: Agreed ₹200 for a 45-minute sunrise boat ride, confirmed verbally twice. On return, the boatman anchored mid-river and demanded ₹2,400 — ₹200 'per person per ghat' we'd passed. Refused, he eventually took ₹400. Agree total price and write it on your phone, show the driver, take a photo of the screen. Witnesses help.

title: Lassi at unnamed stall caused severe disorientation — suspected bhang without disclosure
description: Ordered a plain lassi from a small stall near Manikarnika Ghat. Within 45 minutes had severe disorientation and time distortion consistent with cannabis. Was with a travel companion who got me back to the guesthouse safely. Only order from clearly labelled shops. If you want bhang, go to a licensed bhang shop where it is clearly stated.

title: Phone lifted from front pocket at Colaba Causeway — felt nothing
description: Walking through the narrow Colaba Causeway market lane, phone was in my front jeans pocket. Felt nothing. Noticed it missing 10 minutes later. The technique involves deliberate shoulder jostling from one person while another lifts. Use a crossbody bag with the clasp facing your body. Never put your phone in a pocket in a crowded market.

title: Taxi driver claimed meter was broken — quoted ₹800 for a ₹200 ride
description: Hailed a black-and-yellow outside CST station. Driver said meter was broken and quoted ₹800 to Bandra. Real metered fare is ₹180–220. Refused and took the next cab whose meter worked fine. All Mumbai taxis are legally required to use meters. 'Broken meter' is always a lie. Take the next cab.

title: Fake Elephanta Island ferry sold at Gateway of India — went nowhere near Elephanta
description: Bought what I thought was an Elephanta Island ferry ticket from a man at the Gateway of India pier for ₹350. Boat took me on a 20-minute Harbour cruise and returned. The official MTDC Elephanta ferry ticket is ₹200 and sold only at the orange MTDC booth on the pier. Any other seller is unofficial.

title: Paid ₹45,000 for a 2-week retreat that matched none of its description
description: Booked a 'women-only certified yoga teacher training' for ₹45,000 after a polished Instagram presence. Arrived to find a shared house with no qualified instructor, no women-only environment, and a male 'guru' I'd never seen in the listings. Unable to get a refund. Always research on TripAdvisor and Lonely Planet forums before paying, and never pay more than one week upfront.

title: Rafting operator skipped safety briefing — no life jacket check
description: Booked rafting with an operator who quoted ₹400 versus the ₹650 TURA-registered operators. No safety briefing, life jackets handed out without fit checks, guide had no visible certification. One person in our group fell out at grade 3 rapids. Ended safely but avoidably. Only use TURA-registered operators. Ask: 'Can I see your TURA registration?'

title: Temple 'priest' tied thread on my wrist and demanded ₹2,000 to remove curse
description: Man in saffron near Ram Jhula tied a thread on my wrist while saying a blessing I didn't ask for. Demanded ₹2,000 saying the thread must be tied with a proper puja payment or it would bring bad luck. Walked away with the thread on. It did not bring bad luck. No temple in Rishikesh mandates any donation.

title: Paid ₹1,200 for counterfeit Taj Mahal entry ticket
description: A professionally printed ticket sold by a man 200m before the south gate entrance. The QR code scanned green on his personal phone. At the real gate it failed. Had to buy a second ticket. The counterfeit tickets are very convincing. Buy only at the ASI official window at the gate or at asi.payumoney.com the evening before.

title: Auto driver insisted on one stop — 90-minute marble shop ordeal
description: Pre-agreed ₹300 auto to Agra Fort with explicit 'no stops'. Driver still pulled into a marble inlay showroom claiming it was 'on the route'. Multi-person team inside — one blocks the door, one shows samples, one processes payment. Left after 15 minutes without buying anything. Uber/Ola only in Agra.

title: Cardamom sold as premium grade was mostly stems and husks
description: Bought 500g of 'premium Wayanad cardamom' from a Mattancherry shop for ₹1,800. At home found it was mostly stems and low-grade husks. Genuine cardamom at that price should be full green pods. Buy from shops 2–3 streets back from the main synagogue lane, or from the Kerala government spice store on MG Road, Ernakulam.

title: Auto quoted ₹400 for the ₹5 government ferry route
description: Asked an auto at Ernakulam ferry terminal for Fort Kochi. Driver quoted ₹400 and said the ferry 'wasn't running today'. The ferry was running — I could see it from where I was standing. The government ferry from High Court Jetty to Fort Kochi costs ₹5 and runs every 20–30 minutes until about 9:30pm. Never take an auto for this crossing.

title: DXB customs flagged my SSRI bottle — 90 minutes detained until doctor's letter sent
description: Brought 3 months of sertraline 50mg in original prescription packaging but no doctor's letter. Customs at Terminal 3 pulled me aside, took the bottle, and held me for 90 minutes while a supervisor reviewed. They eventually let me through after I emailed my GP and got a PDF letter on the spot. Common SSRIs are not banned but must arrive with: original packaging + prescription printout + doctor's letter on letterhead stating the diagnosis and daily dose. Without all three you risk confiscation or hours of delay. Apply for a UAE Ministry of Health import permit if carrying more than 3 months supply.

title: Persistent following near Gold Souk after 7pm — three men, two blocks
description: Walked the Gold Souk solo at 7:30pm in long sleeves and trousers. Three different men separately followed me for two blocks each, repeatedly calling out. One blocked my path near the abra stop until I stepped into a jewelry shop. The Gold Souk is fine in daylight (10am–5pm) — after sunset it empties of women shoppers and the dynamic shifts fast. Don't stay in the souks past 6pm solo. Take Careem out from Al Ras metro, not the abra after dark.
location: Sikkat Al Khail Road, Gold Souk, Deira

title: Co-codamol in checked bag = arrest risk at DXB
description: Friend was held overnight at DXB for a 12-pack of co-codamol (codeine + paracetamol) bought over-the-counter in the UK. Codeine is on the UAE controlled substances list — possession without a UAE-issued permit can mean a 4-year prison sentence in extreme cases. Even small amounts have triggered arrests of British and Indian tourists in the past two years. Leave all codeine-containing products at home. Take ibuprofen or paracetamol-only alternatives instead. If you must travel with prescription codeine, apply for UAE MoH approval at least 4 weeks before departure via the eDrug portal.

title: Holding hands flagged by mall security at Dubai Mall — escorted out
description: My partner and I were holding hands walking through Dubai Mall. A security guard followed us for 5 minutes, then asked us to "stop or leave." Same-sex relations are still technically criminalized in the UAE — tourists are rarely prosecuted but public displays of affection can lead to mall removal, hotel staff reporting, or police involvement. Travel as friends in public; book one room with two beds explicitly (a king bed for two women raises questions at check-in); avoid any couple-coded behavior in malls, taxis, and beach clubs. Marina Walk and JBR are more relaxed but still not safe for openly affectionate behavior.

title: Booked 'Marina view apartment' on Airbnb — wrong building, host vanished
description: Booked a 4-night stay in a "Marina view 1BR" via Airbnb. On arrival the address was a sub-leased serviced apartment in JLT, not Dubai Marina — and the photos didn't match the actual unit. Host stopped responding inside the app. Many Dubai short-term rentals are sub-let units where the actual leaseholder doesn't permit Airbnb — a recurring scam pattern in Marina, JLT, and Downtown. Always check: the listing must show a DTCM (Dubai Tourism) license number in the description; cross-reference the building name on Google Maps; pay only via Airbnb (never bank transfer or WhatsApp). Hotels and Booking.com properties are safer for stays under a week.

title: Hospital required marriage certificate for prenatal scan — turned away at ER
description: I was 11 weeks pregnant, travelling with my partner (unmarried). Light bleeding overnight — went to a private hospital ER. Reception asked for a marriage certificate before booking the scan. While the UAE legalised cohabitation in 2020, pregnancy-outside-marriage is still a grey area in some hospitals and can technically result in detention. We were eventually seen after an hour of escalation. If you're pregnant and unmarried: choose a known international hospital chain (American Hospital, Saudi German Clinic, Mediclinic City Hospital — not government clinics), travel with a notarised partnership/single-parent declaration, and do not attempt to give birth in the UAE outside marriage. Travel insurance with maternity coverage is essential.

title: CBD gummies in carry-on = 6 hours detained at DXB arrivals
description: Held for 6 hours at DXB after a sniffer dog flagged a 30-pack of CBD gummies (legal where I bought them in the UK). CBD products of any THC content are illegal in the UAE — possession can mean arrest, even for sealed unopened packs. Same goes for: poppy seeds (poppy-seed bagels and muffins, yes really — multiple arrests on record), kratom, hemp protein powder, any "wellness" supplement with hemp/cannabis derivatives, and certain melatonin products. Empty your luggage of anything CBD-related before flying. Buy any supplements you need after arrival from a UAE pharmacy.

title: Abra captain quoted AED 50 for the AED 1 public ride across the Creek
description: Tried to take the public abra from Bur Dubai to Deira. Captain quoted AED 50 ("private hire only today, no shared service"). The actual government-fixed fare for a shared public abra is AED 1 per person and runs continuously 6am to midnight. Some captains tell tourists the public service is "down," "closed," or "fully booked" to push private hire at AED 100+. Walk to the Bur Dubai Abra Station main pier (not the smaller side docks), look for the queue of locals, and join. If you're quoted more than AED 2 per person, walk to the next pier — they're 2 minutes apart. Never pay the captain before boarding the public service.
location: Bur Dubai Abra Station, Dubai Creek
